title: The Interpreter Who Is Not Supposed to Exist
summary: A deaf patient and her sign-language interpreter disagree about whether a good interpreter should ever be noticed.

body.speaker: Sofia
body.text: Be honest with me, Daniel. In there, with the doctor, did you change anything I signed? It felt like he reacted before I had finished.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: No. I gave him exactly what you said, in the order you said it. My whole job is to be a clear window, not a curtain. You shouldn't even notice I'm there.

body.speaker: Sofia
body.text: That's the part I want to argue with. A window? When the doctor used a word I didn't know, you didn't just translate it. You glanced at me, and I understood that I could ask. A window doesn't do that.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: That glance worries me, actually. We're trained to disappear. The moment an interpreter becomes a person in the room, there's a risk we start deciding what the deaf person needs to know. That's not my decision to make.

body.speaker: Sofia
body.text: But you are a person in the room. Pretending you're invisible doesn't make you neutral. It just hides the choices you're already making. You chose how fast to sign, which word to pick when two could fit. Those are choices, Daniel.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: You're right that there's no perfect, choice-free translation. I think about it constantly. But the danger of admitting that is people then expect me to help, to explain, to take sides. And the day I take your side is the day the doctor stops trusting the channel.

body.speaker: Sofia
body.text: I don't want you on my side. I want you to stop pretending the channel is made of glass when it's made of a human being who got tired in the second hour and started signing faster. I noticed, by the way.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: ...I did. I'm sorry. That's exactly the failure I'm afraid of. If I were truly invisible, my tiredness wouldn't reach you.

body.speaker: Sofia
body.text: But it's not a failure that you're human. It's the reason I trust you over the perfect machine they keep promising us. A captioning app never gets tired, and it also never glances at me to say, 'you're allowed to ask.' I'll take the tired human.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: That's generous. Though I'd still rather be a tired human who is trying to disappear than one who has decided he knows best. The trying matters, even if I never quite reach it.

body.speaker: Sofia
body.text: Then we actually agree, just from opposite ends. You aim to vanish so you don't control me. I want you visible so I can see when you slip. Both of those are really about the same thing: you not taking my voice.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: I hadn't thought of it that way. The invisibility I chase and the visibility you want are both rules for protecting your voice, not mine.

body.speaker: Sofia
body.text: Exactly. So keep trying to be a window. But when the glass cracks, let me see the crack. Don't smooth it over to protect your idea of a perfect interpreter.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: Deal. Next appointment, if I get tired, I'll tell you instead of hiding it. A visible crack you can work around. An invisible one is the dangerous kind.

grammarNotes.title: Should / shouldn't for advice and expectation
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Should' và 'shouldn't' diễn tả lời khuyên hoặc điều được mong đợi.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: "You shouldn't even notice I'm there" expresses how Daniel believes things ought to be.

grammarNotes.title: Second conditional for an unreal present
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu điều kiện loại 2 (If + were, would) nói về tình huống không có thật ở hiện tại.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: "If I were truly invisible, my tiredness wouldn't reach you" imagines an unreal situation.

grammarNotes.title: Gerunds as subjects
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Danh động từ (V-ing) có thể làm chủ ngữ của câu.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: "Pretending you're invisible doesn't make you neutral" uses the -ing form as the subject.

grammarNotes.title: Relative clauses defining people and things
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Mệnh đề quan hệ (who/that) xác định rõ người hoặc vật đang nói đến.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: "a human being who got tired in the second hour" and "the perfect machine they keep promising us" both add defining detail.

translationVi:
Sofia: Hãy thành thật với tôi, Daniel. Ở trong đó, với bác sĩ, anh có thay đổi điều gì tôi đã ký hiệu không? Có cảm giác như ông ấy phản ứng trước cả khi tôi nói xong.
Daniel: Không. Tôi truyền lại cho ông ấy đúng những gì cô nói, theo đúng thứ tự cô nói. Cả công việc của tôi là làm một ô cửa kính trong suốt, không phải tấm rèm. Cô thậm chí không nên nhận ra tôi có ở đó.
Sofia: Đó chính là điều tôi muốn tranh luận. Một ô cửa kính ư? Khi bác sĩ dùng một từ tôi không biết, anh không chỉ dịch nó. Anh liếc nhìn tôi, và tôi hiểu rằng tôi có thể hỏi. Một ô cửa kính không làm vậy.
Daniel: Cái liếc đó thật ra làm tôi lo. Chúng tôi được huấn luyện để biến mất. Khoảnh khắc một người phiên dịch trở thành một con người trong phòng, có nguy cơ chúng tôi bắt đầu quyết định người điếc cần biết điều gì. Đó không phải quyết định của tôi.
Sofia: Nhưng anh đúng là một con người trong phòng. Giả vờ vô hình không khiến anh trung lập. Nó chỉ che giấu những lựa chọn anh vốn đang thực hiện. Anh chọn ký hiệu nhanh đến đâu, chọn từ nào khi hai từ đều hợp. Đó là những lựa chọn, Daniel.
Daniel: Cô nói đúng rằng không có bản dịch nào hoàn hảo, không phải lựa chọn. Tôi nghĩ về điều đó suốt. Nhưng mối nguy khi thừa nhận điều đó là người ta sẽ mong tôi giúp đỡ, giải thích, đứng về một phía. Và ngày tôi đứng về phía cô là ngày bác sĩ ngừng tin tưởng kênh truyền đạt.
Sofia: Tôi không muốn anh đứng về phía tôi. Tôi muốn anh thôi giả vờ rằng kênh truyền đạt được làm bằng kính, trong khi nó được làm từ một con người đã mệt vào giờ thứ hai và bắt đầu ký hiệu nhanh hơn. Tiện thể, tôi đã nhận ra đấy.
Daniel: ...Tôi đã làm vậy. Tôi xin lỗi. Đó đúng là thất bại mà tôi sợ. Nếu tôi thật sự vô hình, sự mệt mỏi của tôi sẽ không chạm tới cô.
Sofia: Nhưng việc anh là con người không phải một thất bại. Đó là lý do tôi tin anh hơn cái cỗ máy hoàn hảo mà họ cứ hứa với chúng tôi. Một ứng dụng phụ đề chẳng bao giờ biết mệt, và nó cũng chẳng bao giờ liếc nhìn tôi để nói, 'cô được phép hỏi đấy.' Tôi chọn con người biết mệt mỏi.
Daniel: Cô thật rộng lượng. Dù vậy tôi vẫn thà là một con người biết mệt mà đang cố gắng biến mất, còn hơn một kẻ đã quyết rằng mình biết rõ nhất. Việc cố gắng mới là điều quan trọng, ngay cả khi tôi chẳng bao giờ thật sự đạt tới.
Sofia: Vậy thì chúng ta thật ra đồng ý với nhau, chỉ là từ hai phía đối lập. Anh nhắm tới việc biến mất để không kiểm soát tôi. Tôi muốn anh hiện hữu để tôi thấy được khi nào anh trượt. Cả hai điều đó thật ra đều cùng về một chuyện: việc anh không lấy đi tiếng nói của tôi.
Daniel: Tôi chưa từng nghĩ theo cách đó. Sự vô hình tôi theo đuổi và sự hiện hữu cô mong muốn đều là những quy tắc để bảo vệ tiếng nói của cô, không phải của tôi.
Sofia: Chính xác. Vậy cứ tiếp tục cố làm một ô cửa kính đi. Nhưng khi tấm kính nứt, hãy để tôi thấy vết nứt. Đừng làm trơn nó đi để bảo vệ ý niệm của anh về một người phiên dịch hoàn hảo.
Daniel: Đồng ý. Buổi hẹn tới, nếu tôi mệt, tôi sẽ nói với cô thay vì giấu đi. Một vết nứt nhìn thấy được thì cô có thể xoay xở quanh nó. Một vết nứt vô hình mới là loại nguy hiểm.

questions.prompt: What does Sofia suspect Daniel may have done during the appointment?
How does Daniel describe his ideal role?
questions.explanation: "My whole job is to be a clear window, not a curtain."

questions.prompt: What action of Daniel's does Sofia point to as proof he is more than a window?
options: He spoke to the doctor privately
He glanced at her so she knew she could ask
questions.explanation: "You glanced at me, and I understood that I could ask. A window doesn't do that."

questions.prompt: Why does that glance worry Daniel?
options: It risks him deciding what the deaf person needs to know
questions.explanation: "we start deciding what the deaf person needs to know. That's not my decision to make."
questions.hint: Daniel's line right after Sofia mentions the glance.

questions.prompt: What is Sofia's argument against the idea of being 'invisible'?
options: Pretending to be invisible hides the choices he is already making
questions.explanation: "Pretending you're invisible doesn't make you neutral. It just hides the choices you're already making."

questions.prompt: According to Daniel, what is the danger of admitting his choices?
options: People will expect him to help, explain, and take sides
questions.explanation: "people then expect me to help, to explain, to take sides."
questions.hint: Daniel's line about 'no perfect, choice-free translation'.

questions.prompt: What did Sofia notice during the second hour?
options: Daniel got tired and started signing faster
questions.explanation: "a human being who got tired in the second hour and started signing faster. I noticed".
questions.hint: Sofia's line that ends 'I noticed, by the way.'

questions.prompt: Why does Sofia say she prefers Daniel over a captioning app?
options: The app never tires but also never glances to say 'you're allowed to ask'
The app is hard to read
questions.explanation: "A captioning app never gets tired, and it also never glances at me to say, 'you're allowed to ask.'"

questions.prompt: What does Daniel mean by 'The trying matters, even if I never quite reach it'?
options: Aiming to disappear is valuable even if perfect invisibility is impossible
questions.explanation: He'd rather be "trying to disappear than one who has decided he knows best".

questions.prompt: What do the two finally realise they agree about?
options: That both invisibility and visibility are really about protecting Sofia's voice
questions.explanation: "both rules for protecting your voice, not mine."
questions.hint: Sofia's and Daniel's lines near the end about 'opposite ends'.

questions.prompt: What does Daniel promise for the next appointment?
options: To change her words for clarity
To tell Sofia if he gets tired instead of hiding it
questions.explanation: "if I get tired, I'll tell you instead of hiding it."

questions.prompt: Which best describes the tone of the conversation?
questions.explanation: They challenge each other honestly but end by finding common ground.
questions.hint: Consider how the argument ends with 'Deal.'

cloze.template:
Sofia: Be honest with me, Daniel. In there, with the doctor, did you change anything I signed? It felt like he reacted before I had finished.
Daniel: No. I gave him exactly what you said, in the order you said it. My whole job is to be a clear {{b1}}, not a curtain. You shouldn't even notice I'm there.
Sofia: That's the part I want to argue with. A window? When the doctor used a word I didn't know, you didn't just {{b2}} it. You glanced at me, and I understood that I could ask. A window doesn't do that.
Daniel: That glance worries me, actually. We're trained to {{b3}}. The moment an interpreter becomes a person in the room, there's a risk we start deciding what the deaf person needs to know. That's not my {{b4}} to make.
Sofia: But you are a person in the room. Pretending you're invisible doesn't make you {{b5}}. It just hides the choices you're already making. You chose how fast to sign, which word to pick when two could fit. Those are choices, Daniel.
Daniel: You're right that there's no perfect, choice-free translation. I think about it constantly. But the danger of admitting that is people then expect me to help, to explain, to take {{b6}}. And the day I take your side is the day the doctor stops trusting the channel.
Sofia: I don't want you on my side. I want you to stop pretending the channel is made of {{b7}} when it's made of a human being who got tired in the second hour and started signing faster. I noticed, by the way.
Daniel: ...I did. I'm sorry. That's exactly the failure I'm afraid of. If I were truly invisible, my {{b8}} wouldn't reach you.
Sofia: But it's not a failure that you're human. It's the reason I trust you over the perfect {{b9}} they keep promising us. A captioning app never gets tired, and it also never glances at me to say, 'you're allowed to ask.' I'll take the tired human.
Daniel: That's generous. Though I'd still rather be a tired human who is trying to {{b10}} than one who has decided he knows best. The trying matters, even if I never quite reach it.
Sofia: Then we actually agree, just from opposite ends. You aim to vanish so you don't control me. I want you visible so I can see when you slip. Both of those are really about the same thing: you not taking my {{b11}}.
Daniel: I hadn't thought of it that way. The invisibility I chase and the visibility you want are both rules for protecting your voice, not mine.
Sofia: Exactly. So keep trying to be a window. But when the glass {{b12}}, let me see the crack. Don't smooth it over to protect your idea of a perfect interpreter.
Daniel: Deal. Next appointment, if I get tired, I'll tell you instead of hiding it. A visible crack you can work around. An invisible one is the dangerous kind.

blanks.explanation: He wants to be "a clear window, not a curtain".
She mocks the idea "the channel is made of glass".
She trusts him over "the perfect machine they keep promising us".
It is about "you not taking my voice".
"when the glass cracks, let me see the crack".

criticalThinkingQuestion: Daniel believes a good interpreter should be invisible, while Sofia argues that pretending to be invisible only hides the choices being made. In a role you know (a translator, a teacher, a referee, a journalist), is it better to aim for invisibility or to be openly visible about your influence? Why?
